ontology.foodex2_description: Potato products and dishes — fried/baked potato preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for potato wedges; A00GJ (potato products) used as closest defensible anchor for fried/baked thick-cut potato preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for potato wedges as a prepared food item; omitted to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of potato wedges is approximately 150g (about 6–8 wedges), commonly served as a side dish or snack alongside a main meal or dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Potato wedges — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for potato wedges (cr_id: F14060011). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Potato, baked/fried preparations — GI value 85 (high); analogous estimate based on fried potato products
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 85 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for fried and baked potato preparations (GI range 70–95 for high-temperature cooked potato products). Potato wedges (deep-fried or oven-baked at high temperature) are classified as high-GI foods. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (85 × 30.51) / 100 ≈ 25.9, based on 150g serving providing approximately 30.51g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 339mg per 100g — borderline high; typical 150g serving delivers approximately 509mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit potato wedge portions to no more than 100g per occasion, request unsalted or lightly salted preparations, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as ketchup or mayonnaise to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) with 20.34g carbohydrate per 100g; glycemic load of approximately 25.9 per 150g serving poses significant postprandial glucose elevation risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should minimise consumption of deep-fried potato wedges due to their high GI and substantial glycemic load; if consumed, limit portions to 80–100g, prefer baked skin-on preparations to reduce GI and increase fibre, and pair with a protein source to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.16g per 100g including saturated fat approximately 1.5g and trace trans fat 0.1g from frying oil; sodium 339mg per 100g adds cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit deep-fried potato wedge consumption due to combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content; baked wedges prepared with minimal oil and no added salt represent a lower-risk alternative, and portion size should be restricted to 100g or less per occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine; high-temperature frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may exacerbate joint inflammation; trace trans fat is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution with potato wedges due to solanine content from the nightshade base, AGE formation from deep-frying, and trace trans fat; baked preparations with turmeric-based seasoning (avoiding paprika, a nightshade spice) are preferable, and individual tolerance to nightshade vegetables should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato wedges are thick-cut sections of whole potato, typically prepared by deep-frying or oven-baking at high temperature, often seasoned with salt, paprika, garlic powder, and other spices. The exterior develops a crispy, golden-brown crust while the interior remains soft and fluffy, delivering a satisfying contrast in texture. They are widely consumed as a snack, side dish, or casual meal accompaniment across Western and international food cultures, and are commonly served with dipping sauces such as ketchup, sour cream, or aioli. Per 100g, potato wedges provide 194 kcal, 3.01g protein, 11.16g fat, and 20.34g carbohydrate with 2.74g dietary fibre and 339mg sodium. The high glycemic index (GI 85) makes them a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, as the glycemic load per 150g serving reaches approximately 25.9. The sodium content at 339mg per 100g places them in the borderline range for hypertension management. Deep-frying introduces saturated fat and trace trans fat, raising cardiovascular concern. Potato is a nightshade vegetable, and high-temperature cooking generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), both of which are relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Baked skin-on preparations with minimal oil and reduced salt represent a nutritionally preferable alternative. Potato wedges are vegetarian, vegan, and generally gluten-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in potato wedges?
Are potato wedges high in sodium?
What is the GI of potato wedges?
How much fat is in potato wedges?
Are baked potato wedges healthier than fried?
Can I eat potato wedges if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14060011) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 85 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published ranges for fried and baked potato preparations. All mandatory fields are populated from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for potato wedges not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, item 11371 — potatoes, baked, flesh and skin) does not provide a directly comparable fried wedge fatty acid profile. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source for this preparation. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this specific potato wedge entry. trans_fat_g of 0.1g per 100g is an estimated trace value consistent with deep-frying in commercial vegetable oils; this is documented in the health_suitability input data. saturated_fat_g of 1.5g per 100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable fried potato preparations (USDA SR Legacy item 11413 — potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated). potassium_mg of 380mg per 100g is referenced from the input health_suitability notes and is consistent with USDA data for cooked potato preparations. GI source is an analogous estimate: no exact GI measurement for potato wedges (fried, thick-cut) was located in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI of 85 is consistent with published high-GI potato preparation values (GI range 70–95) and is retained from the input record. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (85 × 30.51) / 100 ≈ 25.9 based on 150g serving. This record is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied as potato wedges are an international Western food with no mandatory local-language requirement under the cuisine rules.
